meaning: To be unsuccessful in achieving a goal; to not function properly.
example: He failed his driving test twice before passing.
example2: The engine failed halfway up the hill.
example3: I fail to understand why you would do that.
Explanation: 'Fail' is a verb meaning to not succeed, to not meet expectations, or to stop functioning. It's a common but powerful word with negative connotations. It can refer to academic performance (fail an exam), mechanical function (brakes fail), personal expectations (fail to keep a promise), or businesses (business fails). The noun is 'failure'. While generally negative, 'fail' can be constructive when it leads to learning (fail forward). The word is also used idiomatically: 'without fail' (always), 'fail to see' (not understand). It's a fundamental concept in evaluation and performance.
usageTip: Can be transitive (fail a test) or intransitive (the plan failed). Also used with 'to': 'fail to appear'.
commonMistake: Using 'fail' as a noun in formal writing. 'His fail was unexpected' is informal; 'His failure was unexpected' is formal.

meaning: To be unsuccessful in achieving a goal or to stop working properly
example: The experiment will fail if we don't follow the steps.
example2: The engine might fail if it gets too hot.
example3: He didn't want to fail the test, so he studied hard.
Explanation: The word 'fail' can refer to a person's effort or a machine's function. When a person fails, it means they did not reach a required standard. When a system or machine fails, it breaks or stops performing its duty. In the world of technology, 'failure' is often studied to prevent future problems. It is a very common word but can be seen as negative. However, in modern business, 'failing' is sometimes seen as a learning opportunity if handled correctly. It is a direct and powerful verb.
usageTip: Use 'fail' to describe either an unsuccessful attempt or a broken machine.
commonMistake: Using 'fail' as a noun (use 'failure' instead—e.g., 'It was a big failure', not 'It was a big fail').
